svg.text: TAGALOG
PHONOLOGY
TAGALOG CONSONANT SYSTEM
21 phonemes; stops, fricatives, nasals, approximants
Stops: p b • t d • k g • /ʔ/ (glottal stop)
Glottal stop: phonemic, often unmarked in spelling
baˈba (door) vs. báʔba (dip) — stress + glottal distinguish
ng /ŋ/: can occur at word start (unlike English)
TAGALOG VOWELS
Five vowels: a e i o u • /e/ and /i/ sometimes merge
Stress is phonemic: áraw "sun" vs. aráw "day"
Final glottal stop after stressed vowel: bangkóʔ "chair"
STRESS PATTERNS
Stress on penultimate or final syllable (plus glottal-final type)
Stress + glottal stop patterns mark vocabulary meaning
Spanish loanwords: keep original stress (opisina "office")
CEBUANO PHONOLOGY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
CEBUANO CONSONANT SYSTEM
Similar inventory to Tagalog; same Austronesian stock
Glottal stop /ʔ/ also phonemic: kamotʔ "hand" vs. kamot "to scratch"
ng /ŋ/ at word start: ngano "why" • ngil-ad "ugly"
CEBUANO VOWELS
Three contrastive vowels: /a/ /i/ /u/ (more archaic than Tagalog)
Tagalog has 5 vowels • Cebuano has 3 core vowels
[e] and [o] are allophones of /i/ and /u/ in Cebuano
Reflects more conservative Proto-Philippine vowel system
PHONOLOGICAL COMPARISON
Tagalog 5 vowels vs. Cebuano 3 core vowels
Both: phonemic glottal stop • both: /ŋ/ at word start
Both: stress as phonemic • both: CV syllable preference
Cebuano: more archaic vowel system • Tagalog: more vowel distinctions
Both lack tone — unlike mainland SE Asian languages
PLATE 42b ALT · TAGALOG / CEBUANO · PHONOLOGY
